title: Chapter 21: The Stand
content: The world arrives in triplicate.

I slam into Timeline C and Cork City is layered like a botched photograph — wet cobblestones under my feet, yes, but also the gleam of Wall Street's asphalt beneath, and Parisian gravel, and all three exist at once, translucent and bleeding into each other like watercolors left in rain. I see a double-decker bus phasing through a boulangerie phasing through a yellow taxi. The English Market's iron signage flickers between English, French, and a language that might not exist yet.

My knees hit damp brick. My nose gushes — hot copper flooding my upper lip, dripping onto my hands, onto the alley floor. I taste it. I taste the blood and underneath that, something electric, something that tastes the way static looks. The glitching is catastrophic now. No gentle dissolve, no careful step between worlds. This was a car crash of a shift, and my body knows it.
Breathe. Breathe.

I press my palm flat against the wet brick wall. The cold helps. The realness of it — the rough grain, the damp seeping through my sleeve — anchors me to this Cork alley, this one street, this one timeline. The ghost-images of the other worlds shimmer but don't vanish. They never fully vanish anymore.
INTEGRITY: 27%.

The number burns behind my eyes. Four percent gone since I left Julian A standing in that penthouse. Four percent in what felt like seconds. The app is eating through reality like acid through paper, and I'm the one holding the bottle.
Focus. Julian C. Harker is coming for him. That's why I'm here. That's the only reason I get to be anywhere.

A ghost-Wall Street taxi phases through the Cork alley, and I flinch, and the movement sends a fresh wave of nausea rolling through me. I spit blood. Wipe my mouth with my sleeve. Stumble toward the street.

Cork. Just Cork. The English Market's grand entrance looms at the end of the alley, its Victorian ironwork picked out in amber streetlight. Rain has left everything slick and gleaming. A man walks past me on the pavement, umbrella dripping, and he doesn't see the Eiffel Tower fragment hovering above the butcher's shop. Only I do.

I pull out my phone. The Fork app's interface pulses steady and wrong, that amber glow that used to feel warm and now feels like a warning light on a dashboard. I navigate to Julian C's signal — a green dot in the Princes Street area. Moving. Not far.
I run.

The journalist's office is above a bookshop on Paul Street, up a narrow staircase that smells like old paper and instant coffee. I find Julian C standing over a desk cluttered with manila folders, and beside him, a man who looks like he was carved from peat and stubbornness.

Eamon. Grizzled doesn't cover it — he's got the kind of face that's been weathered by fifty years of Irish winters and worse editors. Wire-rimmed glasses. A cardigan with elbow patches that are themselves patched. His hands are thick and ink-stained, and they're shaking as he spreads documents across the desk.
"Everything," Eamon says. His voice is gravel and Guinness. "Julian, I have everything."

Julian C picks up a page. I watch his face as he reads — the way his jaw tightens, the way his eyes go flat and hard. These aren't just files. These are classified military documents, the kind that get people killed, and they're spread across a cluttered desk in Cork like takeaway menus.

"The commanding officer," Julian C says. His voice is quiet. Too quiet. "Coleman. He was on Osprey's payroll for three years before the operation. They owned him. He ordered the strike because they told him to."

"And then they framed you to bury it," Eamon says. "Osprey orchestrated the lot. The cover-up, the court-martial, the evidence that disappeared. All of it. It's all here." He taps the documents. "This clears your name, son."
Julian C's hand trembles. Just once. He sets the page down like it might shatter.

I want to touch him. I want to put my hand on his arm and tell him it's going to be okay, but I don't know that. I don't know anything anymore. And the ghost-images are still flickering at the edges of my vision — a Parisian streetlamp phasing through the window, the distant sound of Wall Street traffic bleeding into Cork's quiet.
Eamon looks at me. Then back at Julian C. His expression shifts — the satisfaction draining out of it, replaced by something older and harder.
"He's not the only one looking for these," Eamon says. "Harker's employers want them too."
The name lands like a punch. Julian C goes still.
"How long?" Julian C asks.

"Minutes. Maybe." Eamon's already moving, pulling a rucksack from under the desk, shoving the documents inside with practiced efficiency. "I've been expecting this. Back exit through the kitchen — there's a passage to the lane behind the chipper."
Glass breaks downstairs.
We all hear it. The clean, deliberate sound of someone who doesn't care about being quiet anymore.
"Go," Eamon says. "Now."
Julian C grabs the rucksack. I grab Julian C's hand — his fingers close around mine, warm and rough and real, the most real thing in this triple-exposed nightmare — and we run.

The back staircase is narrow and dark and I slam my shoulder into the wall at the turn and don't stop. Behind us, from the office above, I hear Eamon's voice rising — not fear, fury. The man is standing his ground. Buying us time.

Julian C pulls me through the kitchen. A woman — the bookshop owner, maybe — screams and presses herself against the counter as we burst past her. Out the back door. Into a lane that smells like chip grease and rain. The cobblestones are slick under my shoes and I nearly go down but Julian C catches me, keeps moving, doesn't break stride.
"This way," he says. Not breathless. Controlled. That military training humming beneath the surface.

We cut left through a gap between buildings so narrow my shoulders nearly touch both walls. Emerging onto Princes Street — and there, fifty meters ahead, a man in a dark coat steps out of a doorway. Professional. Former-military. The way he moves says it without words: trained, efficient, dangerous.

Julian C yanks me right. Over a low wall — I'm climbing before I know I'm climbing, fingers scraping rough stone, the wet leaving streaks on my palms — into a courtyard. Behind the English Market now. I can see its iron gates, locked for the night, the grand Victorian structure looming like a cathedral of commerce.
Two more men at the far end of the courtyard.
They've flanked us.

Julian C's hand tightens on mine. He pulls me through a service door I didn't see — left ajar, a crack of darkness — and we're inside the market, surrounded by the ghosts of daily trade: empty stalls, bolted shutters, the faint smell of fresh fish and artisan bread. Our footsteps echo off the tile floor and there's no way to be quiet, no way —

"Down." Julian C guides me behind a counter, into a crawl space between a cheese stall and a cold storage unit. We crouch. Breathe. His chest is against my back and I can feel his heartbeat, steady as a drum.
Footsteps in the market. Measured. Searching.
My phone glows. I pinch the screen to dim it. The Fork app: INTEGRITY: 26%.

The footsteps pass. Fade. Julian C touches my arm — go — and we move again, out through a service corridor, into a street, across it, through a gateway I'd have walked past a hundred times without noticing. He knows this city. He knows how to disappear in it.

Three more minutes of running. Through alleys that twist like intestines, past pubs that spill light and laughter onto the wet street, over another wall — this one taller, harder, my arms shaking as I haul myself over — and down. Down stone steps into a cellar beneath a pub where the heat hits me like a wall and the smell of stale stout soaks into my clothes.

O'Malley's network. I've never been here but Julian C knows the way. A basement room with a single bare bulb, wooden beams overhead, the ceiling low enough that Julian C has to duck. There's a cot, a kerosene lamp, a bucket. Places to hide people who need hiding.
Julian C closes the door. Bars it. Leans against the wall, chest heaving, and for a moment — just a moment — he lets me see how afraid he is.
Then his face closes off. Soldier mode. He crosses to me, checks my hands — scraped raw from the walls — and cups my face in his palms.
"You're okay," he says. His thumbs brush the drying blood from under my nose. "You're okay, Avery."
I'm not. I'm so far from okay that okay isn't even visible from here. But I nod, because he needs me to.
"They'll find the back exit," I say. "They'll trace us."
"They already have."
The voice comes from above. Through the wooden beams. A footstep on the floorboards overhead. Then another.
Marcus Harker.

"Julian Blake," Harker calls down. Calm. Almost friendly. "You've had your run. I'm not a patient man, Blake. I've been patient anyway, and it's gotten me nothing but wet shoes and a very unhappy employer. Come up. Bring the documents. We can discuss this like reasonable people."

Julian C's expression doesn't flicker. He moves to the door, positions himself between me and the only exit, and that's when I know. That's when I see it — the set of his shoulders, the way he plants his feet. He's going to hold this door. He's going to put himself between me and whatever comes through it.
"When the door opens," he says quietly, "run."
My stomach drops.
"I'll hold them."

The same words. The same goddamn words he said about the teacher in that village — the civilian he shielded with his own body, the act of self-sacrifice that broke his life apart and got him court-martialed. He will do this every time. Every single time. Without hesitation. Without even thinking about it. He will throw himself at danger and take the damage and call it duty.
Something cracks open in my chest. Not heartbreak. Something hotter. Something angrier.
"No," I say.
"Avery —"

"No." I step forward. My hand finds his and grips hard enough to hurt. "Not this time, Julian. You don't get to do this again. You don't get to be the shield every single time until there's nothing left of you."

His brown eyes search mine. I see it — the part of him that believes, truly believes, that his life is worth less than the lives around him. That the only way he has value is as a barrier between danger and someone else. It's written in the lines of his face, in the tension of his grip.
The floorboards creak above. Movement. The door at the top of the stairs rattles.
I release his hand. Pull out my phone.
The Fork app glows amber. INTEGRITY: 27%. The number pulses like a wound.

I've never tried this. I've shifted myself between timelines — that was the whole point, the whole design of the app. I jump. I visit. I observe. I don't pull. I don't drag pieces of other realities into this one like a thief reaching through a window.
But the app has been doing things it wasn't designed to do since the day I downloaded it. Why should this be any different?

Julian A. I focus on him. His mind. The cold, calculating brilliance that built a Wall Street empire from nothing — the tactical brain that sees every variable, every angle, every possible outcome and chooses the optimal path with surgical precision. Julian C has heart. Julian A has strategy. And right now, strategy is what we need.
"Avery, what are you —"

I press my thumb to Julian C's chest. Over his heart. I feel it pounding under my palm, rabbit-fast, alive, too alive to become another sacrifice on the altar of his guilt. And I press the app with my other hand and I PULL.
The world hiccups.

Not a shift — nothing so gentle. This is a tear. A reaching-through-the-fabric moment where I grab something that lives in Timeline A and yank it into Timeline C. I feel Julian A's consciousness like a river current — cold, fast, purposeful — and I redirect it. Not all of it. Not his soul or his self. Just the part that knows how to survive. The tactical mind. The killer instinct.
The electric SHIFT hits me like a defibrillator. My nerves light up. Every cell in my body screams. My vision whites out and then —
Julian C blinks.
His eyes change. Brown bleeds to gray — not a gentle shift but a rapid one, like ink draining from a glass. Arctic gray. Julian A's gray. And then he moves.

The door at the top of the stairs kicks open. A man comes through — former-military, professional, Harker's operative — and Julian C is already moving. Not the Julian C I know, not the man who would throw himself at the threat and absorb the damage. This Julian moves like water through cracks. Precise. Cold. Brilliant.

He takes the first man's arm at the wrist, redirects the momentum, puts him into the wall head-first. The man crumples. The second man is halfway down the stairs, reaching for a weapon, and Julian C is there before the hand closes around it — strips it, reverses it, uses the man's own weight to send him down the remaining stairs in a tangle of limbs. Economy of force. Not violence for violence's sake. Every movement serves a purpose. Every strike ends the threat.
It takes four seconds. Maybe five.
Silence.

The two men are unconscious. Julian C stands between them, breathing hard, and the gray is fading from his eyes — brown seeping back in like warmth returning to a frozen lake. But not completely. Not all the way. There's something left in his gaze that wasn't there before. Something cold, sitting behind the warmth like a stone at the bottom of a river.
He looks at his hands. Turns them over. Studies his own fingers like evidence at a crime scene.
"Avery," he says. But his voice sounds different. Deeper. Flatter. An echo underneath it that I recognize — not from this timeline. "What did you do?"
I can't answer. My hands are shaking. The phone in my grip displays the damage:

INTEGRITY: 23%.
⚠️ TIMELINE COLLAPSE ACCELERATING.
I pulled something from another reality into this one. I merged two versions of the same man. I broke the biggest rule the Fork app ever had, and the app is punishing all three timelines for it.

Julian C looks up at me. Brown and gray. Warm and cold. Two Julians in his eyes, and they're both looking at me, and one of them is grateful and the other is calculating what this means, what I've become, what I'm capable of.

Above us, footsteps. Harker retreating. His men are down and he's smart enough to know when he's lost the immediate exchange. But he hasn't lost the war. I hear his voice, muffled through the floorboards, speaking into a phone. Calling reinforcements. Calling worse.
Julian C puts his hand out to me. Steady. Open. The same hand that just dismantled two trained operatives in five seconds. The same hand that held mine through the streets of Cork.
I take it.
His fingers are cold. They've never been cold before.

Above us, the footsteps fade. And in his eyes, two men are learning to share a single body, and I don't know which one I just saved, and I don't know which one I just damned, and the app is counting down toward zero and I created this.
I created this.
INTEGRITY: 23%.

The collapse is accelerating, and I am the accelerator, and somewhere in the gray behind Julian C's warm brown eyes, Julian A is waking up in a body that isn't his, and the border between timelines is thinning by the second, and I don't know how to stop what I started.
But I know one thing: Julian C is alive. Julian C is standing in front of me with his hand in mine and his eyes full of questions and terrible new answers, and he is alive.

The cost is coming. I can feel it building in the electric hum at the base of my skull, in the triple-exposed ghost-images that won't stop flickering at the edges of my vision. The cost is coming, and it's going to be more than a number on a screen.
But not yet. Not this second. Right now, he's breathing, and I'm breathing, and Harker is gone but not finished, and the documents are safe in the rucksack at Julian C's feet.
Right now, we run.
And we figure out what I've done later.
